content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 6, 1994


Chris Micheli
Tax & Corporate Counsel
California Manufacturers Association
1121 L Street, Suite 900
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-94-134

Dear Mr. Micheli:

You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you on behalf of the California Manufacturers Association
("CMA") concerning the lobbying disclosure provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/

QUESTIONS

1. In a meeting with a public official, would a "contact"
occur for a member of the CMA if he or she is accompanied by CMA's
lobbyist?

2. During a legislative hearing, how many "contacts" will
occur if a CMA member testifies as an expert witness on behalf of
a bill supported or opposed by CMA.

CONCLUSIONS

1. The CMA member would not incur a "contact" for purposes
of qualifying as a lobbyist under Regulation 18239, if the
individual is accompanied by CMA's registered lobbyist and is
acting on behalf of CMA. A "contact" would occur if a CMA member
attempts to influence a public official on an issue unrelated to
CMA.

2. A CMA member testifying at a legislative hearing on one
subject will incur one "contact" regardless of how many
legislators are present.

_________________________________________________________________
1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento, CA 95804-0807● (916) 322-5660

A-93-388
Page 2


FACTS

The California Manufacturers Association employs registered
lobbyists. In addition, the trade association asks its dues-
paying members to testify before legislative committees and to
meet with legislators and their staff regarding legislation of
interest to CMA and its membership.

ANALYSIS

Regulation 18239 provides a "contacts" and "compensation"
test for determining when a person qualifies as a lobbyist. In
order to facilitate application of these tests, the Commission
prepared a memorandum, copy enclosed, which lists common
situations in which the contacts test should be applied and how
many contacts should be counted. The Commission determined that
in most circumstances when a company officer or employee meets
with a legislator along with the company's registered lobbyist,
the primary purpose of the officer or employee's attendance is to
provide technical information on a subject related to the
company's interests. Therefore, a contact was not attributed to
the officer or employee when the registered lobbyist was present.
The Commission has applied the same rationale when a member of an
association accompanies the association's registered lobbyist and
speaks on an issue related to the association's interests.
However, if an association member attempts to influence a public
official on a matter unrelated to the association, a contact
occurs whether or not the association's lobbyist is present.

When an individual testifies at a legislative hearing, the
number of contacts is determined by the number of subjects and
clients involved, not the number of legislators present at the
hearing. See item (3) on page 2 of the enclosed memorandum.

If you have questions concerning this letter, please contact
me at (916) 322-5662.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

by: Lynda Cassady
Assistant Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division

Enclosure: Memorandum to Interested Persons Concerning
Qualification as a Lobbyist Under Section 18239,
dated April 17, 1992.
